text.headline: Geeta Persad — Climate Scientist Chronicle
text.text: A source-backed timeline of education, research formation, policy engagement, faculty career, and scientific recognition.

text.headline: Grew up in Austin, Texas
text.text: Geeta Persad grew up in Austin, exploring the Texas Memorial Museum and attending lectures at UT Austin as a child. Her family originates from Trinidad and Tobago.

text.headline: B.S. in Geophysics, Stanford University
text.text: Earned a Bachelor of Science in Geophysics from Stanford University, leaving Austin for the first time.

text.headline: Physical Scientist, NOAA
text.text: Served as a Physical Scientist at the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration before enrolling at Princeton.

text.headline: Ph.D., Princeton University
text.text: Ph.D. in Atmospheric and Oceanic Sciences from Princeton (2016). NSF Graduate Research Fellow, Ford STEP Fellow, and HMEI-STEP Graduate Fellow. Dissertation focused on atmospheric aerosols and climate impacts.

text.headline: Certificate in Science, Technology, and Environmental Policy — Princeton SIPA
text.text: Earned a certificate in Science, Technology, and Environmental Policy through the HMEI-STEP program at Princeton's School of Public and International Affairs.

text.headline: Consecutive Outstanding Student Paper Awards — AGU
text.text: Received consecutive Outstanding Student Paper Awards from the American Geophysical Union for work on aerosol-climate interactions during her PhD.

text.headline: Research Associate, Carnegie Institution for Science
text.text: Joint postdoctoral position at the Carnegie Institution for Science (Stanford), working with Dr. Ken Caldeira on global climate modeling and aerosol-climate interactions.

text.headline: Senior Climate Scientist, Union of Concerned Scientists
text.text: Led the Western Water and Climate Program at the Union of Concerned Scientists, working directly with government agencies, legislatures, and community stakeholders on Western U.S. water and climate planning.

text.headline: Assistant Professor, UT Austin Jackson School of Geosciences
text.text: Joined the Jackson School of Geosciences at UT Austin as Assistant Professor of Climate Science — returning to her hometown. Leads the Persad Aero-Climate Group.

text.headline: EDF 'Generation Climate' Leader
text.text: Named one of eight 'Generation Climate' leaders by the Environmental Defense Fund, featured on the cover of EDF Solutions magazine. Recognized for work illuminating the role of aerosol pollution in climate change and climate risk worldwide.

text.headline: NOAA Grant — Near-Term Aerosol Climate Risk
text.text: Received a NOAA research grant to study how changing aerosol pollution could influence climate change in the United States in the coming decades.

text.headline: IOP Science paper: Aerosol emissions and near-term climate risk
text.text: Published 'Rapidly evolving aerosol emissions are a dangerous omission from near-term climate risk assessments' in IOP Science.

text.headline: UTIG Seminar — Anthropogenic Aerosol as a Driver of Climate Risk
text.text: Presented at the UT Institute for Geophysics seminar series on anthropogenic aerosol as a driver of spatially complex climate risk.

text.headline: NSF CAREER Award
text.text: Received the prestigious NSF Faculty Early Career Development Program (CAREER) Award for research on how aerosol pollutants contribute to compound climate risks — extreme temperature, rainfall, and humidity occurring simultaneously. Includes a computational field trip education component with the Texas Advanced Computing Center.
